ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for 3-in-1 instant sachet format; closest match is chocolate malt beverage powder dry mix (FOODON_03301099). The 3-in-1 format includes non-dairy creamer and sugar pre-mixed.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (chocolate-flavoured drink powder) is the closest defensible match. The 3-in-1 sachet format with pre-mixed creamer and sugar is not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for instant chocolate malt drink powder sachet. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food product codes at this level.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot or cold water

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared with 200 ml hot water
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 33 g powder) dissolved in 200 ml of hot water, yielding approximately 200 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Milo 3-in-1 Instant Chocolate Malt Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo 3-in-1 sachet (as prepared per 100 g). All nine nutrient fields in the input record carry HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 and are retained as-is. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient fields not reported by HPB for this entry are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Milo (chocolate malt beverage, as prepared) — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate malt beverage drinks
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values in the range of 55 for chocolate malt beverage drinks prepared with water or milk. A GI of 55 is applied here as the closest defensible analogous estimate for Milo 3-in-1 sachet prepared with water. Per the standard GI classification scale (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database): low GI = 55 or below, medium GI = 56–69, high GI = 70 or above. A GI of exactly 55 therefore falls at the upper boundary of the low category and is classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 16.46 g per 200 g serving) / 100 ≈ 9.1. The input record's GL of 4.5 appears to have been calculated on a smaller reference portion; the 200 g prepared serving GL of 9.1 is used here.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.07 g/100 g as prepared) in a sugar-sweetened instant beverage mix; insulin-mediated sodium retention from sugar load overrides the low absolute sodium (38 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Milo 3-in-1 sachets because the added sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk, and the non-dairy creamer likely contributes hydrogenated vegetable fat; unsweetened Milo powder prepared with low-fat milk and no added sugar is a safer alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) classification: 6.07 g sugar/100 g as prepared with added sugar in liquid form, triggering rapid glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo 3-in-1 sachets are classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and are unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management because dissolved sugar in liquid form is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes; even 'less sugar' variants remain SSBs and should be avoided, with unsweetened Milo powder prepared with low-fat milk and no added sugar as the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed SSB with added sugar, likely trace trans fat from hydrogenated non-dairy creamer, and no beneficial omega-3 or dietary fibre above 1 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid Milo 3-in-1 sachets because regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased CVD risk, and the hydrogenated non-dairy creamer component may contribute trace trans fat which is atherogenic at any amount; replacing with unsweetened cocoa or plain low-fat milk is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and likely trace trans fat from hydrogenated non-dairy creamer are pro-inflammatory; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrient (omega-3, polyphenol, fibre) is present in material quantity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Milo 3-in-1 sachets are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because added sugar and hydrogenated vegetable fat in the non-dairy creamer are pro-inflammatory, and no compensating anti-inflammatory factor is present; plain unsweetened cocoa or green tea are preferable beverage choices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milo 3-in-1 Instant Chocolate Malt Drink Sachet is an ultra-processed, pre-portioned single-serve beverage mix produced by Nestlé and widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia. Each sachet contains a blend of malt extract, cocoa powder, sugar, non-dairy creamer, and skimmed milk powder, along with added vitamins and minerals. The product is prepared by dissolving the sachet contents in 200 ml of hot or cold water, requiring no additional ingredients. The resulting drink has a sweet, chocolatey, malty flavour with a creamy texture imparted by the non-dairy creamer. It is a staple breakfast and school-canteen beverage in Singapore and is deeply embedded in local food culture, particularly among Chinese Singaporean households. Nutritionally, as prepared per 100 g, the drink provides 48 kcal, 1.14 g protein, 1.01 g fat, 8.23 g carbohydrate (of which 6.07 g is sugar), 0.48 g dietary fibre, 38 mg sodium, 66 mg calcium, and 1.4 mg iron. The high sugar content relative to total carbohydrate (74% of carbohydrate is sugar) and the liquid delivery format make this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by clinical classification. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low by standard classification), but the rapid absorption of dissolved sugar in liquid form means postprandial glucose impact is clinically significant. The non-dairy creamer component likely contains hydrogenated vegetable fat, contributing trace trans fat. This product is classified as halal and vegetarian but not vegan due to milk-derived ingredients. It is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The product is sold under the Milo brand (美禄 in Chinese) and is known colloquially as 'Milo 3-in-1' or 'Milo sachet' across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Milo sachet?
Is Milo 3-in-1 suitable for high blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of Milo 3-in-1?
What is the GI of Milo?
Can I drink Milo if I have heart disease?
What is the difference between Milo powder and Milo 3-in-1?

confidence_reason: All nine core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food products. The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source, and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: The HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo 3-in-1 sachet does not report detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, or the full micronutrient panel for this product as of the access date. USDA FoodData Central does not carry an exact match for this Singapore-specific product formulation. All -1 values reflect genuine data unavailability in approved sources, not estimation gaps. Note on trans fat: while the HPB entry does not report trans fat, the presence of hydrogenated vegetable fat in the non-dairy creamer component means trace trans fat is likely present; this is flagged in health context guidance. Note on dietary tags: the input record included 'low_calorie' and 'low_sodium' dietary tags; these have been removed from dietary_flags as they are relative marketing claims and not standard dietary classification flags. The halal tag is retained as it reflects certified halal status. GI classification correction (iteration 3): gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low'. Per the standard GI classification scale (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low = 55 or below; medium = 56–69; high = 70 or above). The previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium' to GI 55. GI glycemic load per serving calculated at 9.1 based on 200 g prepared serving (16.46 g carbohydrate per serving); the input record's GL of 4.5 was based on a smaller reference portion and has been superseded.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced from input JSON with HPB nutrient data and University of Sydney GI analogous estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with G5.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. Per standard GI classification (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database), GI = 55 is the upper boundary of the low category (low ≤ 55). The previous iteration incorrectly classified GI 55 as medium. Updated gi_source source_match_notes and notes field to document this correction.
